resumeSimple: The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) is a tool designed to apply a carbon price to imported goods that are carbon-intensive. It aims to ensure that these imports face the same carbon pricing as products manufactured within the European Union. By doing so, it seeks to prevent 'carbon leakage,' where production moves to countries with less stringent climate policies.
enJeu: This mechanism is crucial for maintaining the competitiveness of European industries during the green transition while preventing the relocation of emissions abroad. It also serves as a global instrument in the fight against climate change by incentivizing cleaner production methods internationally.

argumentsPour.point: Ensures importers pay the same levy as domestic industries under the EU ETS, preventing carbon leakage.
Can help maintain European competitiveness during the fundamental climate transition.
Serves as an important new instrument in the global fight against climate change.

argumentsContre.point: Potential negative impacts on industries that are heavily reliant on imports.
Disappointment regarding the lack of sectoral extensions beyond hydrogen in the final agreement.
Lack of mandatory financial support or minimum support levels for least developed countries (LDCs).

chronologie.fait: The European Parliament voted on the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism.

issue: The text was adopted with 487 votes in favour, 81 against, and 75 abstentions.

blocks.titre: Carbon border adjustment mechanism (A9-0160/2022 - Mohammed Chahim)
blocks.resume: The debate concerns the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), a tool designed to ensure that imported carbon-intensive goods face the same carbon pricing as EU domestic products. The discussion focuses on balancing European industrial competitiveness during the climate transition with the need to support developing nations and manage impacts on import-dependent industries.
pointsCles: Equalization of carbon costs between EU producers and importers
Support for least developed countries (LDCs) in the decarbonization process
Scope of sectoral extensions within the mechanism

argumentsPour.point: Ensures importers pay the same levy as domestic industries under the EU ETS, preventing carbon leakage.
Can help maintain European competitiveness during the fundamental climate transition.
Serves as an important new instrument in the global fight against climate change.

argumentsContre.point: Potential negative impacts on industries that are heavily reliant on imports.
Disappointment regarding the lack of sectoral extensions beyond hydrogen in the final agreement.
Lack of mandatory financial support or minimum support levels for least developed countries (LDCs).

orateursCles.role: Supports the mechanism as a tool for competitiveness but warns of risks to import-dependent sectors.
Voted in favour but criticized the loss of EP positions regarding LDC support and sectoral scope.
